Threat.value: I am unable to relax even when I know I am in a safe place
Being superalert or watchful or on guard

vignette:
Mereana, a 46-year-old tattoo artist from Oceania who is single, presents with severe posttraumatic stress symptoms following a sudden life-threatening illness. She describes the illness as a period when “my body just stopped feeling reliable”: one evening she was at home, became acutely unwell, and by the time she reached emergency care she felt she was fighting to stay conscious. The peak danger was the moment she realised she could not predict what her body would do next and had no control over whether she would deteriorate. Since then, she has remained highly wound up, with a persistent startle response and a sense that she cannot fully settle even in places she knows are safe.

When Mereana speaks about the episode, she does so in a way that suggests it is held in mind as an abruptly recurring scene rather than a settled past event. She reports that certain moments “hit me all at once,” and she can be pulled back to the sight of being unwell and frightened in a single vivid surge. At times she stops mid-sentence, appears to scan the room, and says she feels “back in that hospital-feeling” without needing to describe the whole story.

Her central stuck point is that the illness confirmed, in her words, “my body can turn on me without warning, and I can’t trust it to keep me safe.” She also speaks as if vulnerability is now a permanent fact of life: “If it happened once, it could happen again, and I might not get through it.”

In daily life, Mereana notices herself watching for bodily changes much of the time. At work in her tattoo studio, she is especially alert to any odd sensation during long appointments, and she finds it hard to relax even when the day is quiet and a client is settled in the chair. A sudden buzz from equipment, a client shifting unexpectedly, or a sharp noise in the studio makes her jump and look up quickly. She says she keeps “checking myself all the time,” and even when she is home alone she remains on guard, unable to switch off.

The dominant pattern is that her persistent watchfulness keeps the illness close in mind, and the illness in turn keeps her watchfulness switched on. That leaves her tense throughout the day, easily startled, and unable to relax in the very settings where she would usually expect to feel in control. In the studio, this shows up as interrupted concentration, extra difficulty sitting through long sessions with clients, and a constant effort to monitor her own body while continuing to work.
